skill.name: roadmap-planning-expert
skill.description: Guides strategic roadmap planning, sprint planning, milestone estimation, and team capacity planning.
skill.summary: Plan epics, milestones, sprints, capacity, and ClickUp sync workflows.
skill.author: AndresNaza

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported external command and weak cryptography patterns, but review found these are false positives in Markdown guidance. The flagged lines contain code fences, inline file names, and slash-command documentation, with no executable scripts, network calls, prompt injection, or secret access found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Command References
low_findings.description: The external command detections point to a Markdown code fence, inline roadmap file paths, and documented slash commands. These lines describe how a planning skill should organize roadmap files and invoke related commands; they do not execute shell, Ruby, or system commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is plain Markdown documentation with backticks and slash-command names. No executable code, interpreter directive, subprocess API, or user-controlled command construction appears in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography detection points to the YAML description line for roadmap and capacity planning. No hash, cipher, cryptographic API, key handling, or security-sensitive algorithm is present at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is descriptive metadata only. The semantic context confirms there is no cryptographic implementation or recommendation to use a weak algorithm.

content.user_title: Plan Product Roadmaps with Sprint Capacity
content.value_statement: Roadmap plans often fail when epics, milestones, and team capacity are estimated separately. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code create practical sprint and milestone plans.
actual_capabilities: Explains a roadmap model based on quarters, epics, milestones, and sprints.
Applies a capacity model of 20 points per developer per two-week sprint.
Guides collaborative planning for quarters, epics, and sprint allocation.
Defines when to split milestones larger than 25 points.
Documents roadmap file locations under a .roadmap directory.
Describes ClickUp sync concepts for epics, milestones, and status updates.
limitations: Does not include executable automation or scripts.
Does not track pull requests, QA status, or detailed issue execution.
Assumes a two-week sprint and a 20-point developer capacity model.
ClickUp syncing depends on separate commands outside this skill.

use_cases.title: Plan a Quarterly Roadmap
use_cases.description: Convert product goals into epics, milestone sizes, and sprint placement for a six-sprint quarter.

use_cases.title: Estimate Delivery Capacity
use_cases.description: Calculate whether planned milestones fit available team capacity with a practical buffer.

use_cases.title: Prepare Sprint Scope
use_cases.description: Select milestones for an upcoming sprint based on team availability and known work size.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Roadmap
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a new initiative
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a quarter for this product goal. Ask me for missing details, then propose epics, milestones, estimates, and sprint allocation.

prompt_templates.title: Break an Epic into Milestones
prompt_templates.prompt: Break this epic into two to four milestones. Estimate each milestone in points and explain which milestones should be split.

prompt_templates.title: Check Sprint Capacity
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this sprint plan against our team capacity. Use a 15 percent buffer and identify which milestones should move.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Roadmap and Sync Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a quarterly roadmap with epics, milestones, sprint placement, dates, and a ClickUp sync mapping for each planned work item.

output_examples.input: Plan a quarter for a billing system rewrite with three developers.
output_examples.output: A six-sprint roadmap with epics for billing foundation, migration, customer workflows, and reporting, each sized against available capacity.

output_examples.input: Our sprint has two developers and 42 points of proposed work.
output_examples.output: A capacity review showing the sprint is overloaded after buffer, with recommendations to defer lower-priority milestones.

output_examples.input: Break the mobile onboarding epic into milestones.
output_examples.output: A milestone plan covering account setup, permissions, guided setup, and analytics, with point estimates and split recommendations.

best_practices: Provide team size, sprint length, and planned absences before capacity planning.
Keep milestones coarse enough for roadmap planning but specific enough to assign to sprints.
Review milestone estimates with the delivery team before committing dates.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill as a replacement for detailed issue tracking.
Do not force milestones larger than 25 points into a single sprint.
Do not plan a full quarter without reserving buffer for unexpected work.

faq.question: What planning method does this skill use?
faq.answer: It uses quarters, epics, milestones, and two-week sprints with a simple point-based capacity model.

faq.question: Can it manage detailed engineering tasks?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on strategic planning and expects detailed execution tracking to happen in tools such as GitHub or ClickUp.

faq.question: How does it estimate capacity?
faq.answer: It assumes one developer has 20 points per two-week sprint and recommends a 15 percent buffer.

faq.question: Does it create roadmap files automatically?
faq.answer: No. The skill documents the expected roadmap file structure but does not include executable file creation code.

faq.question: Can it help with ClickUp?
faq.answer: It describes how epics and milestones map to ClickUp, but syncing depends on separate ClickUp commands.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: Product managers, engineering managers, and planning leads can use it to shape realistic roadmap commitments.
